247	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over a field \(k\). There exists a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(J\) over \(k\) of relative dimension \(g(C)\) such that, for every \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\), the scheme \(J\) is an Albanese object for the pointed curve \((C, P)\) in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}. Equivalently, the type of Albanese witnesses for \(C\) (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) is non-empty.
248	\end{theorem}
249	\begin{proof}
250	  The proof is split into three classical constructions plus the genus-\(0\) sub-case absorbed into the same witness (the latter packaged as the explicit construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}, which routes through the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} --- rigidity proved over the algebraic closure \(\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k\) --- and then \emph{descends} the resulting morphism equality from \(\bar k\) to the base field \(k\) along the faithfully-flat surjection \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\)). Each route produces the same intrinsic scheme \(J\); only the universal morphism \(\iota_P \colon C \to J\) varies with the marked point \(P\). We outline each route and name the Mathlib infrastructure each one requires.
251	  \paragraph{(0) Reduction to a \(P\)-independent witness.}
252	  The witness \(J\) depends only on \(C\), not on the marked point \(P\). Indeed, both classical constructions below produce \(J\) as an intrinsic object of \(C\) (the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\), or the Stein-factorisation target of \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) translated by a fixed \(P_0\)); a different choice of \(P\) in \(C(k)\) alters \(\iota_P\) by a translation of \(J\), which is an automorphism, and so does not change \(J\) as a scheme. The Lean encoding reflects this by bundling \(J\) as a single field and the universal-property data as \(\forall P,\, \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\, C\, P\, J\). We now turn to producing \(J\).
253	  \paragraph{Route A --- Picard scheme.}
254	  This route follows Hartshorne~III.4 and FGA Explained, Chapter~9.
255	  \begin{itemize}
256	    \item[\textbf{(A.1)}] \emph{The relative Picard functor.} For a \(k\)-scheme \(T\), write \(C_T := C \times_k T\) and let \(\pi \colon C_T \to T\) be the projection. The relative Picard functor is
257	    \[
258	      \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k} \colon (\Sch/k)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \Sets, \qquad
259	      T \longmapsto \Pic(C_T) / \pi^*\Pic(T).
260	    \]
261	    A \(T\)-point is a line bundle on \(C_T\) modulo a line bundle pulled back from \(T\). The set-valued formulation suffices for representability; the canonical abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C_T)\) descends to the quotient by \(\pi^*\Pic(T)\) and gives \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}\) a natural group-functor refinement.
262	    \item[\textbf{(A.2)}] \emph{Representability.} For \(C\) smooth proper geometrically connected over \(k\) with a \(k\)-rational point, \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}\) (after \'etale sheafification, if \(k\) is not algebraically closed) is representable by a \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), locally of finite type. This is FGA's main representability theorem (Grothendieck, FGA~232); the smooth-proper-geometrically-connected hypothesis ensures that the cohomology-and-base-change machinery applies and that \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = k\), which is needed for the quotient by \(\pi^*\Pic(T)\) to be \'etale-locally injective.
263	    \item[\textbf{(A.3)}] \emph{The identity component.} Let \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) be the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\). As the identity component of a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is geometrically irreducible; for \(C\) smooth proper of genus \(g\), it is moreover a smooth proper \(k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\). Smoothness comes from the deformation-theoretic identification of its tangent space at the identity with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) and homogeneity of the group action; properness from the fact that the natural map \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\) is an open and closed immersion onto a proper component (a consequence of the cohomological criterion: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the kernel of the degree map \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k\), which is locally constant). Take \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
264	    \item[\textbf{(A.4)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi universal morphism.} For each \(P \in C(k)\), the Abel--Jacobi morphism
265	    \[
266	      \iota_P \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}, \qquad
267	      Q \longmapsto [\mathcal O_C(Q - P)]
268	    \]
269	    sends \(P\) to the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). It is universal among morphisms \(f \colon C \to A\) to smooth proper geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group schemes \(A\) with \(f(P) = \eta_A\): this is the classical Albanese property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), proved by reducing to the case \(k = \overline k\) (Galois descent), where it is a standard consequence of the autoduality of the Jacobian and the fact that line bundles on \(C \times A\) trivialised along \(\{P\} \times A\) correspond bijectively to morphisms \(C \to A^{\vee} = A\) sending \(P\) to \(\eta_A\).
270	  \end{itemize}
271	  \emph{Mathlib status for Route A.} Each sub-step is missing.
272	  \begin{itemize}
273	    \item Sub-step A.1 requires a working theory of relative line bundles on a product \(C \times_k T\) and the descent of the abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\) along \(\pi^*\). The pieces are present individually but not assembled. (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 700\)--\(1100\) LOC, \(\sim 6\)--\(10\) iters.)
274	    \item Sub-step A.2 requires FGA representability for \(\Pic_{C/k}\), which in turn requires Hilbert / Quot scheme infrastructure not in Mathlib (\texttt{HilbertScheme}, \texttt{QuotScheme}, and the cohomology-and-base-change for proper morphisms with finitely presented kernels). The construction engine for this Quot/Hilbert layer is Nitsure, ``Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes'' (FGA Explained; arXiv:math/0504590), \S\,4 (generic flatness and the flattening stratification) and \S\,5 (the construction of \(\mathrm{Quot}\), hence \(\mathrm{Hilb}\), as a scheme); see \texttt{references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.md}. (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 2200\)--\(3000\) LOC, \(\sim 15\)--\(25\) iters --- the dominant Route A block.)
275	    \item Sub-step A.3 requires connectedness machinery for group schemes locally of finite type, the identity-component construction \(G^0 \subseteq G\), and the degree map \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k\). (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 600\)--\(900\) LOC, \(\sim 5\)--\(8\) iters.)
276	    \item Sub-step A.4 requires the universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), which is the classical Albanese functoriality and is not in Mathlib in this form. \textbf{Bypass via Milne autoduality FAILS}: as Milne III \S~6 writes the proof of Proposition~6.1, it invokes Theorem~3.2 (rational-map-extension from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety) directly to upgrade the symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular map \(J \to A\), and the would-be Picard-functor / autoduality detour \(J \cong J^\vee\) routes through the theorem of the cube, excised from this project iter-163. Sub-step~A.4 therefore inherits the Theorem~3.2 + Lemma~3.3 codim-\(1\) extension + Weil-divisor API + Auslander--Buchsbaum sub-build (the last absent from Mathlib). (See iter-172 audit at \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP}, NOTE block + \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} proof.) (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 2500{+}\) LOC, \(\sim 22\)--\(35\) iters.)
277	  \end{itemize}

2113	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
2114	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
2115	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
2116	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
2117	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
2118	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
2119	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
2120	  \[
2121	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
2122	  \]
2123	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
2124	  abelian-group structure with addition
2125	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2126	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
2127	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
2128	  inverse
2129	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
2130	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
2131	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2132	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
2133	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
2134	  map
2135	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
2136	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
2137	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2138	\end{theorem}
2139	
2140	\begin{proof}
2141	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2142	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2143	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2144	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2145	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2146	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2147	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
2148	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
2149	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
2150	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
2151	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
2152	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
2153	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
2154	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
2155	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
2156	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
2157	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
2158	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
2159	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
2160	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2161	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
2162	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
2163	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
2164	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
2165	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
2166	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
2167	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
2168	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
2169	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
2170	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
2171	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
2172	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
2173	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
2174	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
2175	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
2176	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
2177	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
2178	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
2179	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
2180	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
2181	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
2182	
2183	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
2184	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
2185	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
2186	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
2187	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
2188	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
2189	
2190	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
2191	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
2192	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
2193	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
2194	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
2195	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
2196	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
2197	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
2198	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
2199	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2200	\end{proof}

2344	\section{Sheaf internal-hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (the \(\otimes\)-inverse
2345	  infrastructure)}
2346	\label{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}
2347	
2348	The PRIMARY group law (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) needs a
2349	\(\otimes\)-inverse for every line bundle, supplied by
2350	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} as the dual
2351	\(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\). The standard route
2352	constructs that dual as a special case of the \emph{sheaf internal hom} of
2353	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; over a fixed ring the internal hom of \(R\)-modules is the
2354	linear-coyoneda module \(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,N = (M \to N)\), with dual
2355	\(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,\mathbf{1} = M^\vee\), but for the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
2356	no such
2357	internal-hom / monoidal-closed structure exists at the
2358	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\), \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\), or general-categorical
2359	level. This section decomposes the construction of the missing object into
2360	individually-formalizable sub-steps, following the slice presentation of the internal
2361	hom; each block names the intended Lean declaration and its dependencies. The
2362	specialisation that closes \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is recorded in
2363	\cref{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}; an alternative descent-theoretic route is noted in
2364	\cref{rem:dual_via_stack}.
2365	
2366	A structural point governs the whole construction. The naive rule
2367	\(U \mapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) is \emph{contravariant} in the
2368	restriction maps of \(M\) (a restriction \(M(U) \to M(V)\) for \(V \subseteq U\)
2369	induces precomposition \(\Hom(M(V), N(V)) \to \Hom(M(U), \cdot)\) in the wrong
2370	direction), so it is \emph{not} a presheaf in the covariant sense and cannot be
2371	sheafified like the tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The remedy is
2372	the \emph{slice} formula \(U \mapsto (M|_U \to N|_U)\) --- the module of morphisms of
2373	\emph{restricted} modules over the restricted ring --- whose presheaf functoriality
2374	(restrict a global-over-\(U\) morphism to a global-over-\(V\) morphism, for
2375	\(V \subseteq U\)) \emph{is} covariant. Constructing that slice presheaf, and
2376	proving it satisfies the sheaf condition, is the substance of the build.
2377	
2378	\begin{definition}
2379	
2380	\leanok
2381	[The global-ring module on morphisms of presheaves of modules (value module)]
2382	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2383	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.homModule}
2384	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2385	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2386	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2387	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3508--L3514).
2388	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2389	  % "abelian groups. In addition, given an element
2390	  % $\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2391	  % and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
2392	  % $f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2393	  % by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
2394	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
2395	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
2396	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2397	  (tag area 01CM): the section ring acts on a morphism of restricted modules by
2398	  post-composition with multiplication by the section.}
2399	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a base category \(\mathcal{C}\)
2400	  that has a terminal object \(T\), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\).
2401	  The abelian group of morphisms \(M \longrightarrow N\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
2402	  carries a canonical \(R(T)\)-module structure --- the \emph{morphism module}
2403	  \(\mathtt{homModule}\,M\,N\). A global scalar \(f \in R(T)\) acts on a morphism
2404	  \(\varphi : M \to N\) by post-composition with multiplication by \(f\),
2405	  \[
2406	    f \cdot \varphi \;:=\; m_f^{N} \circ \varphi,
2407	  \]
2408	  where \(m_f^{N} : N \to N\) is the \emph{multiplication-by-\(f\)} endomorphism of
2409	  \(N\): the endomorphism that, on each object \(U \in \mathcal{C}\), is multiplication
2410	  by the restriction \(f|_U \in R(U)\) of \(f\) along the unique morphism \(U \to T\)
2411	  to the terminal object. The module axioms hold because the assignment
2412	  \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\) is a ring homomorphism \(R(T) \to \mathrm{End}(N)\) into the
2413	  endomorphism ring of \(N\) --- a product \(f \cdot g\) goes to the \emph{composite}
2414	  \(m_f^{N} \circ m_g^{N}\) of the two multiplication endomorphisms, the order in which
2415	  the factors compose being reversed by the fact that the scalar acts by
2416	  \emph{post}-composition --- and composition of morphisms is biadditive in the
2417	  preadditive category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). Distributivity and the
2418	  associativity of the action then follow from bilinearity of composition together with
2419	  the homomorphism property of \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\). This is the genuinely new core of
2420	  the construction: Mathlib supplies the \emph{fixed}-ring internal hom
2421	  \(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,N = (M \to N)\) over a single base ring, but nothing equipping the
2422	  morphism group with the module structure for the \emph{varying} structure presheaf at
2423	  the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
2424	\end{definition}
2425	
2426	\begin{definition}
2427	
2428	\leanok
2429	[Slice specialisation of the morphism module]
2430	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
2431	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.internalHomObjModule}
2432	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2433	  Specialise \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} to a slice category. For an object
2434	  (open) \(U\) of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\), restricting along the forgetful functor
2435	  \(\mathtt{Over.forget}\,U : \mathcal{C}/U \to \mathcal{C}\) --- the open-immersion
2436	  restriction \(M \mapsto M|_U\) --- carries \(M, N\) to presheaves of modules
2437	  \(M|_U, N|_U\) over the restricted structure ring on the slice \(\mathcal{C}/U\). The
2438	  slice \(\mathcal{C}/U\) has the terminal object \(\mathtt{Over.mk}\,(\mathrm{id}_U)\),
2439	  at which the restricted ring takes the value \(R(U)\); hence
2440	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} makes the morphism group
2441	  \(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\) an \(R(U)\)-module, the
2442	  \(\mathtt{internalHomObjModule}\). This \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) is exactly
2443	  the value \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}.
2444	\end{definition}
2445	
2446	\begin{definition}
2447	[Presheaf internal hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
2448	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2449	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.internalHom}
2450	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:presheaf_internal_hom_value,
2451	        def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value, lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
2452	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2453	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2454	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3500--L3524).
2455	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2456	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2457	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2458	  % Consider the rule
2459	  % $$
2460	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2461	  % $$
2462	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2463	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2464	  % abelian groups. In addition, given an element
2465	  % $\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2466	  % and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
2467	  % $f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2468	  % by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
2469	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
2470	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2471	  % We will denote this sheaf
2472	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})$.
2473	  % There is a canonical ``evaluation'' morphism
2474	  % $$
2475	  % \mathcal{F}
2476	  % \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
2477	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
2478	  % \longrightarrow
2479	  % \mathcal{G}.
2480	  % $$"
2481	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2482	  (tag area 01CM).}
2483	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on \(\Opens X\) and let
2484	  \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). The \emph{presheaf internal hom}
2485	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules whose value on an open
2486	  \(U\) is the module of morphisms of restricted modules
2487	  \[
2488	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)
2489	      \;:=\;
2490	    \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\;\bigl(R(U)\bigr)\;\bigl(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\bigr),
2491	  \]
2492	  the \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear morphisms \(M|_U \to N|_U\) of presheaves of
2493	  modules on \(U\), formed via the open-immersion restriction \(U \hookrightarrow X\).
2494	  This value module is the slice specialisation
2495	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value} of the morphism module
2496	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}.
2497	
2498	  Explicitly, \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules assembled from
2499	  three pieces. \emph{(a) Value modules.} On each open \(U\) the value is the
2500	  \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} /
2501	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}. \emph{(b) Restriction maps.} For an
2502	  inclusion \(V \subseteq U\) the restriction map
2503	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \to \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V)\) is the further-restriction map
2504	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}, \(\varphi \mapsto \varphi|_V\), which
2505	  is additive and semilinear over the ring restriction \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\)
2506	  induced by \(g : V \to U\) --- the compatibility datum that the presheaf-of-modules
2507	  assembly (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.mk}\), via \(\mathtt{ofPresheaf}\)) consumes.
2508	  \emph{(c) Functoriality.} Restriction along the identity \(U \to U\) is the identity
2509	  map, and restriction along a composite \(V \xrightarrow{g} U \xrightarrow{h} W\) of
2510	  inclusions \(V \subseteq U \subseteq W\) (the morphism \(g : V \to U\) followed by
2511	  \(h : U \to W\) in \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\)) is the composite of the two
2512	  restriction maps \(\mathcal{H}om(M,N)(W) \to \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(U) \to
2513	  \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(V)\); both identity and composition are inherited from the
2514	  functoriality of further restriction, since \(\mathtt{Over.map}\) is a functor. These three pieces
2515	  make \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) a genuine, covariant presheaf of \(R\)-modules; this
2516	  assembled presheaf is the construction target \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.internalHom}\).
2517	
2518	  The slice formula is forced by contravariance: the pointwise rule
2519	  \(U \mapsto \Hom_{R(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) varies \emph{against} the restriction maps of
2520	  \(M\) and so is not a covariant presheaf, whereas the module of morphisms of
2521	  \emph{restricted} objects \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricts covariantly. Thus, unlike the
2522	  tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} --- which is covariant in the
2523	  restriction maps and sheafifies directly --- the internal hom must be built through
2524	  the slice presentation, and there is no presheaf-level dual to sheafify naively.
2525	\end{definition}
2526	
2527	\begin{lemma}
2528	
2529	\leanok
2530	[Restriction map of the presheaf internal hom]
2531	  \label{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
2532	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.restrictionMap}
2533	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value, def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
2534	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2535	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2536	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3508).
2537	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2538	  % "Consider the rule
2539	  % $$
2540	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2541	  % $$
2542	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2543	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2544	  % abelian groups."
2545	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2546	  (tag area 01CM): the rule \(U \mapsto \Hom(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)\), with its
2547	  further-restriction maps, is a (pre)sheaf of abelian groups.}
2548	  Let \(g : V \to U\) be a morphism of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\) (an inclusion
2549	  \(V \subseteq U\) of opens), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). There
2550	  is a restriction map
2551	  \[
2552	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \longrightarrow \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V),
2553	    \qquad \varphi \longmapsto \varphi|_V,
2554	  \]
2555	  the \(\mathtt{restrictionMap}\), sending a morphism of restricted modules
2556	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to its further restriction \(M|_V \to N|_V\). Concretely, the
2557	  functor \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g : \mathcal{C}/V \to \mathcal{C}/U\) realises
2558	  ``restrict from \(U\) to \(V\)'', and \(\varphi|_V\) is \(\varphi\) pulled back along
2559	  it --- the further restriction of the same morphism of presheaves of modules. This
2560	  map is additive, and it is \emph{semilinear} over the ring restriction
2561	  \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\): for \(f \in R(U)\),
2562	  \[
2563	    (f \cdot \varphi)\big|_V \;=\; R(g)(f) \cdot \bigl(\varphi|_V\bigr).
2564	  \]
2565	  This semilinearity is the compatibility datum that the presheaf assembly of
2566	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} (through \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf}\) /
2567	  \(\mathtt{mk}\)) requires.
2568	\end{lemma}
2569	
2570	\begin{proof}
2571	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2572	  Further restriction along \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g\) is functorial, so it carries a
2573	  morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to a morphism \(M|_V \to N|_V\) and respects identities and
2574	  composites; in particular the induced map on morphism groups is additive. For the
2575	  semilinearity, recall that the global-scalar action of
2576	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} acts on \(\varphi\) by post-composition with
2577	  the multiplication-by-\(f\) endomorphism \(m_f^{N}\); restricting that endomorphism
2578	  from \(U\) to \(V\) is multiplication by \(f|_V = R(g)(f)\) on \(N|_V\). Since further
2579	  restriction commutes with composition of morphisms, restricting \(f \cdot \varphi =
2580	  m_f^{N} \circ \varphi\) yields \(m_{R(g)(f)}^{N|_V} \circ (\varphi|_V) = R(g)(f) \cdot
2581	  (\varphi|_V)\), which is the asserted compatibility.
2582	\end{proof}
2583	
2584	\begin{definition}
2585	[Presheaf dual against the structure presheaf]
2586	  \label{def:presheaf_dual}
2587	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dual}
2588	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2589	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as in \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}, the
2590	  \emph{presheaf dual} of \(M\) is the internal hom into the unit (structure)
2591	  presheaf,
2592	  \[
2593	    M^\vee \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om(M, R),
2594	  \]
2595	  i.e.\ \(M^\vee(U) = \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\,(R(U))\,(M|_U \to R|_U)\), the
2596	  \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\). This is the
2597	  presheaf-of-modules analogue of the linear dual
2598	  \(\mathtt{Module.Dual}\,R\,M = (M \to R)\) that serves as the \(\otimes\)-inverse of
2599	  an invertible module over a fixed ring; here the fixed-ring dual is taken
2600	  open-by-open and assembled by the slice presentation of
2601	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}.
2602	\end{definition}
2603	
2604	\begin{lemma}
2605	[Evaluation/contraction of the internal hom]
2606	  \label{lem:internal_hom_eval}
2607	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.internalHomEval}
2608	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2609	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2610	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2611	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3517--L3524).
2612	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2613	  % "There is a canonical ``evaluation'' morphism
2614	  % $$
2615	  % \mathcal{F}
2616	  % \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
2617	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
2618	  % \longrightarrow
2619	  % \mathcal{G}.
2620	  % $$"
2621	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2622	  (tag area 01CM).}
2623	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as above, there is a canonical \emph{evaluation} (contraction)
2624	  morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
2625	  \[
2626	    \mathrm{ev}_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_R M^\vee \;\longrightarrow\; R,
2627	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
2628	  \]
2629	  the counit of the tensor--hom adjunction
2630	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M \otimes_R -, R) \cong \mathcal{H}om(-, M^\vee)\) specialised at
2631	  \(R\). On each open \(U\) it is the open-by-open contraction
2632	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R|_U} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R|_U\), \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto
2633	  \phi(s)\), and these are compatible with restriction, so they assemble to a morphism
2634	  of presheaves of modules. The tensor product is the presheaf tensor underlying
2635	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
2636	\end{lemma}
2637	
2638	\begin{proof}
2639	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2640	  On a fixed open \(U\), the value \(M^\vee(U) = (M|_U \to R|_U)\) is the module of
2641	  \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\), and the evaluation
2642	  \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto \phi(s)\) is the standard contraction
2643	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\) bilinear over \(R(U)\), hence a
2644	  well-defined \(R(U)\)-linear map out of the tensor product. For \(V \subseteq U\)
2645	  the square relating the \(U\)- and \(V\)-contractions commutes because evaluating
2646	  then restricting equals restricting both arguments then evaluating
2647	  (\(\phi(s)|_V = (\phi|_V)(s|_V)\)); hence the open-by-open contractions are natural
2648	  in the restriction maps and define a morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. This
2649	  is the counit of the tensor--hom adjunction of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2650	  specialised to the unit presheaf.
2651	\end{proof}
2652	
2653	\begin{lemma}
2654	[The internal hom is a sheaf; the sheaf-level dual]
2655	  \label{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}
2656	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual}
2657	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
2658	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2659	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2660	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3514).
2661	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2662	  % "Consider the rule
2663	  % $$
2664	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2665	  % $$
2666	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2667	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2668	  % abelian groups. ... Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
2669	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2670	  (tag area 01CM).}
2671	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), and let
2672	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with \(N\) a sheaf of modules. Then the
2673	  presheaf internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2674	  satisfies the sheaf condition: for an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\), a family of
2675	  morphisms \(M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) agreeing on the overlaps
2676	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) glues to a unique morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\), because \(N\) is a
2677	  sheaf and morphisms of sheaves of modules are determined and gluable section-wise.
2678	  Hence \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) descends to an object of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
2679	  Specialising to \(N = \mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,R\) gives the
2680	  \emph{sheaf-level dual}
2681	  \[
2682	    \mathtt{dual}\,M \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X)
2683	    \;\in\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X,
2684	  \]
2685	  and the evaluation \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval} descends to a morphism of sheaves of
2686	  modules \(M \otimes_X \mathtt{dual}\,M \to \mathcal{O}_X\).
2687	\end{lemma}
2688	
2689	\begin{proof}
2690	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
2691	  Fix an open \(U\) and an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\). A morphism
2692	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) is a section of \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) over \(U\); restricting it
2693	  to the \(U_i\) gives a compatible family. Conversely, given morphisms
2694	  \(\varphi_i : M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) with \(\varphi_i|_{U_i \cap U_j} =
2695	  \varphi_j|_{U_i \cap U_j}\), section-wise gluing in the sheaf \(N\) produces, for
2696	  each open \(W \subseteq U\) and section \(s \in M(W)\), a well-defined section of
2697	  \(N(W)\) by gluing the \(\varphi_i(s|_{W \cap U_i})\) (these agree on overlaps by
2698	  hypothesis, and \(N\) is a sheaf so the glued section is unique). The resulting
2699	  assignment is \(R(W)\)-linear and natural in \(W\), hence a morphism
2700	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricting to the \(\varphi_i\); uniqueness is the separatedness
2701	  of \(N\). This is exactly the sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\), so it is a
2702	  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Taking \(N = \mathcal{O}_X\) yields
2703	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,M\), and the evaluation morphism of \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval},
2704	  being a morphism of presheaves into the sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\), is a morphism of
2705	  the corresponding sheaves of modules.
2706	\end{proof}
2707	
2708	\begin{lemma}
2709	[The dual of a line bundle is a line bundle]
2710	  \label{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}
2711	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_isLocallyTrivial}
2712	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
2713	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
2714	  % modules), Lemma~lemma-constructions-invertible (item~2)
2715	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200--L4213).
2716	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2717	  % "\item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
2718	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
2719	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
2720	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
2721	  % is an isomorphism."
2722	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR,
2723	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (item~2).}
2724	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with
2725	  \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\). Then the sheaf-level dual
2726	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} is again locally trivial of
2727	  rank one, i.e.\ \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,L)\).
2728	\end{lemma}
2729	
2730	\begin{proof}
2731	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
2732	  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an open \(U\) with a
2733	  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The internal hom commutes with
2734	  open-immersion restriction --- the slice formula
2735	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} is defined through \(L|_U\), so
2736	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(L|_U, \mathcal{O}_U)\),
2737	  the restriction-compatibility being the dual analogue of
2738	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} for the internal hom. Under the trivialisation,
2739	  \[
2740	    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U
2741	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
2742	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U,
2743	  \]
2744	  the last identification being evaluation at \(1\), the canonical iso of the dual of
2745	  a free rank-one module with itself. Hence \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) is locally trivial of
2746	  rank one. (Equivalently, this is item~2 of Stacks
2747	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible}, tag 01CR: the dual of an invertible
2748	  module is invertible.)
2749	\end{proof}
2750	
2751	\begin{remark}
2752	[How the dual infrastructure discharges the \(\otimes\)-inverse]
2753	  \label{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}
2754	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial,
2755	        lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
2756	  Once \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom,def:presheaf_dual,lem:internal_hom_eval,%
2757	  lem:internal_hom_isSheaf,lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} are available,
2758	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is discharged with no further missing
2759	  object: take \(L^{-1} := \mathtt{dual}\,L\) (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}), which
2760	  is a line bundle by \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}, and take \(\varepsilon_L\) to
2761	  be the descended evaluation of \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval}. On a trivialising open
2762	  \(U\), the restriction-compatibility iso \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} carries
2763	  \((L \otimes_X L^{-1})|_U\) to \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U\) and
2764	  \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) to the contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to
2765	  \mathcal{O}_U\) of the free rank-one module with its dual, which is the left unitor
2766	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and hence an isomorphism. As ``being an isomorphism''
2767	  is local on \(X\), the global \(\varepsilon_L\) is an isomorphism
2768	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\) by
2769	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} together with the structure-sheaf-unit comparison
2770	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}. This is the realization the
2771	  ``infrastructure-blocked'' note in \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} points
2772	  to.
2773	\end{remark}
2774	
2775	\begin{remark}
2776	[Alternative route via object-level descent (fallback)]
2777	  \label{rem:dual_via_stack}
2778	  Should the slice-and-sheafify route of
2779	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom,lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} bottom out, the dual can
2780	  instead be assembled by \emph{object-level descent}: glue the local trivial duals
2781	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) along the inverse-transpose transition isomorphisms
2782	  \(g_{ij}^{-\mathsf{T}}\) of \(L\) into a global \(L^{-1}\) on \(X\). Abstractly this
2783	  is effective descent for sheaves of modules on the small Zariski site --- the
2784	  essential-surjectivity of the descent-data functor of an \(\mathtt{IsStack}\)
2785	  pseudofunctor \(U \mapsto (\mathcal{O}_U\text{-modules})\). This is the more
2786	  principled and reusable construction but the larger build (it requires constructing
2787	  the restriction pseudofunctor and proving the stack/effective-descent property),
2788	  and is recorded only as a fallback; the slice internal-hom route above is the
2789	  intended one. It is the same morphism-/object-level descent family on which the
2790	  associator re-route of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} also waits.
2791	\end{remark}

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51

53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}

57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.

32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
36	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
37	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
38	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
39	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
40	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
41	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
42	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
43	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness; see~\)
44	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
45	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.

40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54

27	\section{Tangent space at the identity}
28	\label{sec:pic0_tangent_space}
29	
30	For a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type, the tangent
31	space at the identity \(\tu T_0 G\) is the \(k\)-vector space of
32	\(k\)-derivations of its local ring at \(e\) into \(k\), or
33	equivalently (and functorially) the set
34	\(G(k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2))_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-points
35	whose underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). For the Picard scheme of a
36	geometrically integral projective \(k\)-scheme, this tangent space is
37	identified with the first sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf via
38	the truncated exponential sequence and the étale-sheaf comparison
39	theorem; on a smooth proper curve, this is the genus-many-dimensional
40	\(k\)-vector space \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\).

1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, \(k\) denotes a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) of relative dimension \(1\). In other words, \(C \to \Spec k\) is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\); see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\) is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the \(k\)-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of \(C\).
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
30	
31	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
32	    The formal definition uses the project's \(\ModuleCat k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
33	    \[
34	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
35	    \]
36	    The carrier \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules constructed from \(\struct C\) via the structure morphism; the wrapper \(\HModule k F 1\) is the \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\), which carries a canonical \(\Module k\)-structure inherited from the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category.
37	  \end{remark}
38	
39	  Mathlib's \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) returns the \(k\)-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme guarantees \(\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty\), so \(g(C)\) is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
40	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology with \(k\)-module coefficients}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_MayerVietoris}
3	
4	This chapter constructs the Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology valued in \(k\)-modules, and develops the \v{C}ech-acyclicity machinery for affine basic-open covers. Together these provide the categorical engine for the Serre finiteness argument (Phase~A step~6). The carriers \(H^n(X,\mathcal F)\) and \(H^{\prime n}(U,\mathcal F)\) live in Chapter~REF and are imported here.
5	
6	The chapter introduces no protected declarations: every definition, lemma, and instance here is auxiliary, supporting the construction of \(H^1(C,\mathcal O_C)\) in Definition~REF.
7	
8	\section{Functoriality of cohomology in the open variable}
9	\label{sec:hmodule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
10	
11	The cohomology-of-an-open carrier \(H^{\prime n}(X,\mathcal F)\) is contravariantly functorial in the open variable \(X\). Assembling this functoriality yields a presheaf
12	\[
13	  X \longmapsto H^{\prime n}(X, \mathcal F),
14	\]
15	and, varying \(\mathcal F\) as well, a bifunctor from sheaves of \(k\)-modules to presheaves of abelian groups. The codomain is the category of abelian groups because \(\Ext\) is always valued there, regardless of the linear enrichment of the source category; the per-fiber \(k\)-module structure is inherited from the reducible wrapper on \(H^{\prime n}\).
16	
17	\begin{definition}\leanok
18	[Cohomology presheaf functor, \(H'\) flavor]
19	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheafFunctor}
20	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_cohomologyPresheafFunctor}
21	  
22	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a category equipped with a Grothendieck topology \(J\), and assume sheafification and \(\Ext\) exist for sheaves of \(k\)-modules on \(J\). For \(n \in \mathbb N\), define the cohomology presheaf functor
23	  \[
24	    \mathcal H^{\prime n}(k, J) \;:\; \Sheaf\,J\,(\ModuleCat\,k) \longrightarrow (C^{\op} \longrightarrow \AddCommGrpCat)
25	  \]
26	  by the standard composition of the Yoneda embedding, the free-\(k\)-module functor, sheafification, and the \(n\)-th \(\Ext\) functor.
27	\end{definition}
28
